reviews.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the ioctl implementation needs to handle compressed extents correctly and agreed to address this in a future version.

The author addressed a concern about the likelihood of collisions in the deduplication logic, explaining that due to the birthday paradox, a 50% chance of collision occurs every 256 MB for 32-bit hashes and is negligible for larger hash sizes.

Author acknowledged that the ioctl interface is not set in stone and can be changed if needed, but emphasized its current speed benefits over manual calculation.
sentiment_signals: agreed to address in a future version
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks David.

On 14/04/2026 3.07 am, David Sterba wrote:

---

IIRC the mathematics of the birthday paradox is that for an n-bit hash, 
you need 2^(n/2) sectors to have a 50% chance of a collision.

For the 32-bit hashes, that means a 50% chance of a collision every 256 
MB. For the 256-bit hashes, that's 2^140 bytes... whatever that works 
out as. So the dedupe logic would no doubt be that for larger hash sizes 
GET_CSUMS itself will be sufficient.

---

That's fine - this isn't an interface change, so we can always change it 
later if it turns out to be a bottleneck. At any rate it's going to be 
faster than userspace calculating the csums manually.

2026-04-15.patch_summary: This patch adds a new unprivileged ioctl, BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows userspace to query the on-disk checksums for a file without exposing sensitive information about inaccessible files. The ioctl takes a struct btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_args with offset and length details, and writes a struct btrfs_ioctl_get_csums_entry into a provided buffer along with the checksums if available. This is primarily used to speed up mkfs.btrfs --rootdir by avoiding recalculating checksums for files that already have them stored on disk.
